sections.title: Stop the Bleed Protocol
sections.content: Stop the Bleed is a national emergency response that teaches bystanders how to control severe bleeding before professional help arrives. Severe bleeding can cause death in 5 minutes or less, so immediate action is critical.

sections.title: When to Use Stop the Bleed, Use this protocol if you see:
items: Blood that is spurting or pumping
Loss of part of a limb
A victim who is pale, confused, or unconscious after injury

steps.title: Scene Safety & BSI (Body Substance Isolation)
steps.subtitle: A safety practice used to prevent exposure to blood and other potentially infectious materials. Ensure scene safety, apply gloves and eye protection. Check for hazards. Protect yourself so you can safely help others.

steps.title: Assess the Bleeding
steps.subtitle: Identify bleeding source and severity. Look for arterial vs venous bleeding.

steps.title: Direct Pressure
steps.subtitle: Put on gloves if available, apply firm direct pressure over bleeding site with gauze, clean cloth or clothing. Press firmly and continuously on the wound with both hands. If blood soaks through, do not remove the first dressing, add more on top.

steps.title: Elevate if Possible
steps.subtitle: Elevate injured area above heart level if no spinal injury suspected.

steps.title: Pressure Points
steps.subtitle: If bleeding continues, apply pressure to arterial pressure points.

steps.title: Tourniquet Application
steps.subtitle: Apply a Tourniquet (For Severe Limb Bleeding). Place the tourniquet 2–3 inches above the wound (not over a joint), tighten until bleeding stops completely. Note the time applied, Do NOT loosen once applied. If no commercial tourniquet is available, use a belt if available, or use a wide band of cloth and a windlass (stick/rod) to tighten.

steps.title: Monitor & Document
steps.subtitle: Monitor vital signs, document time of interventions, prepare for transport.
